title: Kapusta z grochem
subtitle: Polish sauerkraut with yellow split peas
excerpt: Sauerkraut braised slowly with yellow split peas, onion, and allspice until the peas collapse into a thick, earthy mash. A Lenten staple across Poland, vegan, sustaining, and much better than it sounds.
sourceNotes: Kapusta z grochem (sauerkraut with peas) is one of the oldest dishes in Polish cooking, associated with Lent and with Wigilia (Christmas Eve), when meat is excluded from the table. The combination of fermented cabbage and dried legumes is found across Eastern European peasant cooking and predates the regular availability of meat. The dish is complete without any animal products.

content.text: This dish is one of the quiet pillars of Polish peasant cooking, two inexpensive, shelf-stable ingredients that together produce something warming and complete. The split peas dissolve as they cook, thickening the sauerkraut brine into a dense, savoury mash. The allspice and bay leaves are not decorative: they pull the dish together in a way that neither ingredient does on its own.

Rinse the split peas before using but do not soak them. They cook quickly enough without soaking, and pre-soaking makes them harder to collapse fully. The sauerkraut goes in partway through cooking, not at the start, so it retains some texture against the soft peas.

items.name: Sauerkraut
items.prepNote: drained, roughly chopped if in long strands

content.text: Warm the {{sunflower-oil}} in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 8 minutes until soft and golden. Add the {{split-peas-yellow}}, {{allspice}}, {{bay-leaves}}, and enough cold water to cover by 5 cm. Bring to a simmer and cook for 25 minutes, stirring occasionally.

Add the {{sauerkraut}} and {{caraway-seeds}} if using. Stir to combine and continue simmering for 25 to 30 minutes more, until the peas are completely soft and beginning to collapse. The mixture should be thick, more mash than soup. Remove the bay leaves and allspice berries. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Serve hot with bread.

items.fix: Continue cooking and top up with hot water as needed. Older peas can take up to 80 minutes to fully collapse
items.cause: Old dried peas, or hard water interfering with softening

items.fix: Simmer uncovered on medium heat for 10 more minutes, stirring frequently to prevent sticking. The peas will thicken it
items.cause: Too much water added, or not cooked uncovered long enough

items.fix: Add a pinch of sugar to balance. Rinsing the sauerkraut briefly before using reduces acidity, but also loses some flavour, rinse only half if you want to moderate rather than eliminate the sourness
items.cause: Sauerkraut very acidic or not drained enough

content.text: Kapusta z grochem is a Lenten dish and a Wigilia staple, the Polish Christmas Eve meal, which is meatless by tradition. It appears alongside herring, fish, pierogi with mushroom, and red borscht. Outside of those occasions, it is weekday food: warming, cheap, and needing nothing beside bread. The flavour deepens considerably on the second day, and it reheats well with a splash of water.
